ontology.foodex2_description: Pork, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for the braised pork component of this composite dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite code for Braised Pork Rice Set. A0CKP (cooked pork) is used as the primary protein anchor. The rice base and sauce components are not separately coded here.

allergens: gluten (wheat in soy sauce and oyster sauce)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Braised Pork Rice Set is approximately 350 g, comprising a bowl of white rice (approximately 200 g cooked) topped with braised pork belly slices and braising sauce, typically served with a hard-boiled egg and a small portion of preserved vegetables or blanched greens.

nutrient_source.title: Braised Pork Rice Set — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100g nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Braised Pork Rice Set (cr_id G00010392). All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB entry and is set to -1. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are not reported in the HPB entry and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (boiled, Asian-style)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Braised Pork Rice Set as a composite dish. GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component: boiled white rice (Asian-style), which has a well-established GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., 2021). The braising sauce and pork belly components contribute negligible additional carbohydrate and are not expected to materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 88.2) / 100 ≈ 63.5, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 25.19 × 3.5 = 88.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from soy-based braising sauce; composite per-100g figure of 231 mg sodium likely underestimates true per-serving sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to the high sodium content from soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and oyster sauce used in braising; if consumed, request sauce on the side and limit portion size to reduce sodium intake well below 400 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~72) with 25.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g; pork belly saturated fat worsens insulin resistance; braising sauce adds sugar to glycaemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit this dish due to its high glycaemic load (estimated GL ~63.5 per standard serving); substituting brown rice, reducing rice portion, and requesting reduced-sugar braising sauce are recommended strategies to lower postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Pork belly is high in total fat (4.21 g/100 g composite) with significant saturated fat contribution; high sodium from braising sauce elevates blood pressure risk; cholesterol reported as 0 mg/100 g in HPB composite but pork belly inherently contains dietary cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to the saturated fat content from pork belly and the high sodium load from soy-based braising sauce, both of which contribute to elevated LDL cholesterol and arterial blood pressure; leaner protein substitutes and reduced-sodium preparation are strongly advised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork belly is high in saturated fat and arachidonic acid (omega-6), both pro-inflammatory; high sodium from braising sauce worsens RA-related tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this dish as pork belly's saturated fat and arachidonic acid amplify inflammatory pathways, and the high sodium from soy-based braising sauce exacerbates joint-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Pork Rice Set (卤肉饭套餐; Lu Rou Fan Tao Can) is a popular Singaporean Chinese hawker and coffeeshop dish consisting of steamed white rice topped with slow-braised pork belly slices and a rich, savoury braising sauce made from soy sauce, dark soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, five-spice powder, and sugar. The set typically includes a soy-braised hard-boiled egg and a portion of preserved mustard greens or blanched leafy vegetables. The pork belly is braised until tender, yielding a melt-in-the-mouth texture with a deeply umami, slightly sweet, and aromatic flavour profile. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate, with 231 mg sodium and 3.7 g dietary fibre. The dish has a high glycaemic index (~72) driven by the white rice base, and a high glycaemic load per standard serving (~63.5 GL units for a 350 g plate). Clinically, this dish is a concern for individuals with type 2 diabetes (high GI and GL, sugar in braising sauce), hypertension (high sodium from soy-based sauce), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat from pork belly, high sodium). It is not halal due to pork content. The dish is a staple of Singaporean Chinese cuisine, widely available at hawker centres, coffeeshops, and food courts across Singapore, and is commonly consumed at lunch and dinner.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Braised Pork Rice Set (cr_id G00010392), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component (boiled white rice) using the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source. Confidence is high for nutrient values; GI is an analogous estimate clearly documented.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, fat-soluble vitamins, B-vitamins, and moisture data for this composite Singaporean braised pork rice set are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026. 2. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g in the HPB composite record; this is preserved as reported but noted as potentially underestimated given pork belly's inherent cholesterol content — the composite averaging across the full set (rice, pork, egg, vegetables) may dilute the cholesterol figure. 3. Sodium of 231 mg/100 g is the HPB composite figure; actual per-serving sodium from soy sauce and oyster sauce in braising is likely higher than this composite suggests. 4. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on boiled white rice (Asian-style) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. (2021); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources. 5. The dish contains pork and is not halal. 6. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03311069 is used as the closest approximate match for a braised pork dish concept; no exact FoodOn entry for Braised Pork Rice Set exists. 7. FoodEx2 code A0CKP (cooked pork) is used as the closest approximate anchor; no composite dish code for this set meal exists in FoodEx2.
